coredata.dc:title: Lignin derived multicolor carbon dots for visual detection of formaldehyde
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ying
Hu, Miaoyan
Liu, Kai
Gao, Shiyu
Lian, Hailan
Xu, Changyan
coredata.dc:description: Formaldehyde (FA) is a common toxic pollutant in air and water, which is harmful to human’s health. Although the fluorescent probes based on carbon dots (CDs) for FA detection have been reported, most of them only work under UV excitation conditions. It is a challenge to develop a CDs probe which can detect the FA more directly without UV excitation. In this work, a series of lignin derived multicolor carbon dots (M-CDs) from blue to cyan and to green emission were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Polychromatism of CDs was successfully achieved by adjusting the amount of hydrochloric acid (HCL) in the hydrothermal process. The blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity towards FA in dual modes. Without the UV excitation, the B-CDs probe was able to detect the FA by observing with naked eyes, and the LOD in sunlight was 0.4 mg/L. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of B-CDs can be quenched by FA, and it correlated linearly with FA concentration from 0.02 mg/L to 1 mg/L with LOD of 7.40 μg/L. This work is in favor of developing the lignin derived CDs with multi fluorescent color and simplifies the method of formaldehyde detection based on CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Formaldehyde (FA) is a common toxic pollutant in air and water, which is harmful to human’s health. Although the fluorescent probes based on carbon dots (CDs) for FA detection have been reported, most of them only work under UV excitation conditions. It is a challenge to develop a CDs probe which can detect the FA more directly without UV excitation. In this work, a series of lignin derived multicolor carbon dots (M-CDs) from blue to cyan and to green emission were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Polychromatism of CDs was successfully achieved by adjusting the amount of hydrochloric acid (HCL) in the hydrothermal process. The blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity towards FA in dual modes. Without the UV excitation, the B-CDs probe was able to detect the FA by observing with naked eyes, and the LOD in sunlight was 0.4 mg/L. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of B-CDs can be quenched by FA, and it correlated linearly with FA concentration from 0.02 mg/L to 1 mg/L with LOD of 7.40 μg/L. This work is in favor of developing the lignin derived CDs with multi fluorescent color and simplifies the method of formaldehyde detection based on CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Formaldehyde (FA) is a very important synthetic material in modern chemical industry. With good water solubility and strong reducibility, it is widely used in the preparation of phenolic resin (Flueckiger et al., 2009), plastics (Chen et al., 2017), detergent (Lachapelle and Tennstedt, 1981), pharmaceuticals, etc. Indoor furniture and other decorative materials can emit free FA throughout life, and it can also be found in drinking water, rainwater, and industrial wastewater. However, as a colorless substance with strong pungent smell, formaldehyde can cause irreversible damage to the central nervous system, immune system and respiratory system of human body exposed to FA environment for a long time (Zhang et al., 2009; Chen and Yuan, 2015). In 2006, the classification of FA was upgraded from probably carcinogenic to carcinogenic to humans by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) (Allouch et al., 2013b). The maximum allowable level of FA in indoor air determined by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) is 80 ppb (Yao et al., 2020) and 750 ppb (Guo et al., 2018), respectively. In the China's National Indoor Air Quality Standard is 0.1 mg/m3. China's National Standard for drinking Water Quality set the permissible limit of formaldehyde in drinking water as 0.9 mg/L. Moreover, FA was included in the list of toxic and harmful water pollutants in 2019 by the Environmental Protection Bureau. To meet the increasingly stringent requirements of formaldehyde pollution control, the accurate, rapid, convenient and intuitive detection of free formaldehyde has attracted extensive attention from industry and academia.

Gas chromatography, high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical analysis and spectroscopy are the commonly used methods for formaldehyde detection in academic research at present (Bunde et al., 2000; Zhu et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2014; Gu et al., 2010; Antwi-Boampong and BelBruno, 2013; Liu et al., 2005; Allouch et al., 2013a; Chung et al., 2014). Yeh et.al. used gas chromatography-mass spectrometry to investigate free and reversibly bound formaldehyde in squid products (Yeh et al., 2013). Yilmaz et.al. developed a simple high-performance liquid chromatography method for the determination of formaldehyde in human tissue. And the limits of detection and quantification of formaldehyde were 1.5 and 5.0 mu g/mL, respectively (Yilmaz et al., 2016). Menart et.al. developed a disposable electrochemical sensor for the detection of FA. It exhibited a linear response in the examined concentration range of 4–16 ppm gaseous formaldehyde in combination with 120 min accumulation (Menart et al., 2017).

However, in practice, these methods cannot meet the needs of the public due to high cost and time consuming. In recent years, FA fluorescent probe has gradually entered the researchers’ view because of its simple operation, low cost, high sensitivity and good selectivity (Martínez-Aquino et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018; Yusuf et al., 2022). A variety of small molecule fluorescent probes for FA detection have been reported (Wu et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2020). Besides, carbon-based nanomaterials have also been used to detect FA (Wang et al., 2020b; Suresh and Bandosz, 2018). As a new member of the carbon nanomaterials family, carbon dots (CDs), discrete quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with particle size less than 10 nm, has become the new star of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials since its discovery in 2004 due to its high solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and controllable fluorescence (Xu et al., 2004; Xu et al., 2021). Theoretically, CDs has a good potential as a fluorescent substance for fluorescent probes because it can be functionalized for different functional requirements. In fact, researchers have successfully used carbon dots to detect thiram (Chu et al., 2020), amines (Zhang et al., 2020), tetracycline (Hu et al., 2021), Cr3+ (Jia et al., 2021), Fe3+ (Xu et al., 2020), Hg2+ (Yan et al., 2021), ethanol (Sun et al., 2022), pyrimethanil (Supchocksoonthorn et al., 2021), etc. In contrast, the study on the use of carbon dots as fluorescent substance for FA detection is just starting, and the relevant research results are only sporadic public reports. Li et al. (2018a) reported a kind of redox induced CDs which was sensitive to FA. Liu et al. (2020b) combined CDs with transparent wood as a building material for real-time detection of FA gas. Liu et al. (2019) detected the FA in living cells with lysosome-targeted CDs. Qu et al. (2020) and Wang et al. (2020b) also applied CDs to detect FA in cells. In the above scattered reports, CDs fluorescent probe can detect FA, which is present in air, water, or living cells. However, those probes need to be excited by ultraviolet light to play the role of FA detection, and the detection results cannot be directly observed by eyes. Therefore, they are not convenient to use, and the detection results are not accurate because different observers have subjective assumptions about the fluorescence color, brightness and saturation of color. Therefore, it is of great academic and practical value to design a fluorescent probe based on CDs that can accurately display FA content levels in sunlight without UV excitation.

In this study, lignin was chosen as the precursor of carbon source to synthesize CDs. Lignin, a renewable substance exist widely in plants, is up to 30 % in the wood especially (Luo and Abu-Omar, 2018). Unfortunately, lignin is often dumped as waste along with industrial effluents or used as inefficient fuel because of its hard degradation (Zhu et al., 2021b). Even so, lignin contains lots of aromatic rings in its structure, which makes it suitable for the preparation of CDs. Some researchers have already prepared the CDs with lignin, such as Li (Li et al., 2018b), Shi (Shi et al., 2019), Zhang (Zhang et al., 2019), Zhu (Zhu et al., 2021a), Yang (Yang et al., 2021) and so on. But few reports studied on the synthesis mechanism and the fluorescent mechanism of the CDs derived from lignin. We believe that the discussions between lignin and CDs are valuable and instructive for the synthesis and functionalization of lignin based CDs. M-phenylenediamine (MPDA) was introduced to functionalize the lignin derived CDs, because the most popular method to make a fluorescent probe capable of detecting FA is to introduce amino groups onto its surface (Liu et al., 2020a). In addition, hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs because of its advantages of low cost, eco-friendly, simple operation and uniform particle size of CDs prepared (Gao et al., 2021). Considering the difficult degradation of lignin, hydrolytic polymerization of lignin was accelerated by hydrochloric acid (HCL) (Nada et al., 2002). At first, we synthesized a series of multicolor CDs (M-CDs) by hydrothermal method using lignin, MPDA and different amounts of HCL as raw materials to explore the effect of depolymerization degree of lignin on the properties of prepared M-CDs. Then, blue emitting CDs (B-CDs), cyan emitting CDs (C-CDs) and green emitting CDs (G-CDs), which present obvious different fluorescence in the prepared M-CDs, were chosen to reveal the relationship of particle size, crystallinity, and elemental composition of M-CDs on its fluorescence properties, and to reveal the synthesis mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of lignin M-CDs. Finally, B-CDs was applied to detect FA in water. The results show that B-CDs has good sensitivity and high selectivity to FA, and it can realize the visual detection of FA.

Lignin (120 mesh) was purchased from Taobao. MPDA, formaldehyde solution (FA, 37 %), HCL (36–38 %), 0.1 M sulfuric acid and quinine sulfate were obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ was used in all the experiments.

M-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method (Xu et al., 2021) according to the formula in Table S1 and the process was shown as Fig. 1. For B-CDs, 0.4 g lignin and 0.4 g MPDA were mixed with 60 mL deionized water (initial reaction solution) and transferred into a Teflon-lining (100 mL) with stainless steel autoclave. Then the autoclave was placed into an electric oven at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the reaction mixture was firstly purified with filtration membrane (0.22 µm), then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and finally purified with dialysis bag (1000 Da) to obtain B-CDs solution. In order to synthesize cyan emitting CDs (C-CDs) and green emitting CDs (G-CDs), different amount of HCL were used to adjust the fluorescent emitting of lignin derived CDs. Specifically, at the same hydrothermal condition (180 °C, 10 h), 20 μL and 1000 μL hydrochloric acid were added into the initial reaction solution above for C-CDs and G-CDs respectively. The powder of B-CDs, C-CDs or G-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying the CDs solution at − 65 °C under vacuum for 3 days.

The scheme of formaldehyde detection using the prepared lignin-based carbon quantum dots is shown in Table S2. First, formaldehyde was added into B-CDs (10 mg/mL) solution and the mixture was placed at room temperature for 1 min. Then, a fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to evaluate the sensitivity of the B-CDs fluorescence probe to FA. The emission intensity of B-CDs with different contents of formaldehyde was recorded under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm UV light. The limit of detection (LOD) of the B-CDs fluorescence probe to FA was calculated by formula 1 (Gao et al., 2021).
(1) LOD = 3σ/s
Where σ means the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, and s stands for the slope of the linear curve fitted.

In addition, the selectivity of the B-CDs probe towards FA in water was also evaluated by adding different interferences into B-CDs, including K+, Zn2+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+. Then the selectivity of the probe towards FA was obtained by calculating the values of F/F0 excited at 380 nm (F and F0 are the fluorescent intensity of B-CDs and B-CDS with interferences). The stability of B-CDs under different pH conditions were measured by adding B-CDs solution into different solution with different pH value (3−9).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100 UHR) was used to investigate the morphology of the prepared CDs. The M-CDs’ images of X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained on a combined multifunctional horizontal X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV, Japan) and an AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK), respectively. The M-CDs’ spectra of FT-IR and Raman were collected on a VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) and a Laser Raman spectrometer (Themor, DXR532, USA), respectively. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum (UV–vis) of the M-CDs was carried out by a Lambda 950 UV-Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA), and its photoluminescent (PL) emission and excitation spectra were obtained on a LS 55 instrument (PerkinElmer, USA). Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were measured under the optimized excitation wavelength using a FLS 980 time-correlated single-photon counting system (EI, UK).

Quantum yield (QY) was calculated via the formula (2) (Xu et al., 2021). Quinine sulfate, which was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid, was used as the reference with PLQY of 54 % (excited at 360 nm).

A series of M-CDs were synthesized according to the formula in Table S1. Herein, B-CDs is lignin derived CDs without HCL, which performs blue fluorescence ( Fig. 2a); whereas G-CDs with plenty of HCL (1 mL) shows green fluorescence (Fig. 2c). Between B-CDs and G-CDs, as the amount of HCL decreases (Table S1), the M-CDs shows blue-green fluorescence from blue to green. For example, C-CDs with 20 μL HCL presents cyan fluorescence ( Figs. 2b), 3-CDs shows light green and 4-CDs is dark green (Fig. S1). B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs with obvious different fluorescent colors were chosen to reveal the relationship of particle size, crystallinity, and elemental composition of M-CDs on its fluorescence properties, and to reveal the synthesis mechanism and fluorescence mechanism of lignin M-CDs. Fig. 2a-c shows the UV–vis absorption spectra, excitation spectra, emission spectra and fluorescent images of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs. At 365 nm excitation, each of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs exists double maximum emission peaks at around 430 nm and 500 nm (Fig. 2a-c) with different intensity. However, as the increase of HCL load, the emission peak at 430 nm becomes weaker, especially for G-CDs; at the same time, the emission peak at 500 nm becomes higher, especially for G-CDs. This experimental phenomenon corresponds to the fluorescent color change of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs in Fig. 2d-f, where the CIE chromaticity coordinates of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs locate at (0.17, 0.18), (0.18, 0.24) and (0.27, 0.54), respectively. In the UV–vis absorption spectra of M-CDs (Fig. 2a-c), the absorption peaks at 230 nm and 290 nm are due to π-π * transitions of CC bond (Zheng et al., 2021) and n-π * transitions of CO bond (Zhao et al., 2021), respectively. Notably, the CC bond indicates that the prepared M-CDs possesses conjugated structure (Zhao et al., 2021; Kailasa and Koduru, 2022). Comparing the two absorption peaks of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs, the red shift occurs from (230 nm, 286 nm) (B-CDs) to (236 nm, 292 nm) (C-CDs) and further to (243 nm, 292 nm) (G-CDs), which suggests that the increased conjugation can decrease the energy gap of electron transition.

In addition, the fluorescent center of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs can be easily found in 3D fluorescent spectra (Fig. 2g-i). It is notable that B-CDs possesses one fluorescent center at (340 nm, 424 nm); whereas, C-CDs has two fluorescent centers at (340 nm, 424 nm) and (440 nm, 512 nm), and G-CDs also shows two fluorescent centers at (340 nm, 422 nm) and (440 nm, 512 nm). From B-CDs to G-CDs with the decrease of HCL from 0 to 1000 μL, the fluorescent center at (340 nm, 424 nm) gradually diminishes; on the other hand, the intensity corresponding to the fluorescent center at (440 nm, 512 nm) gradually enhances. It reveals how the fluorescent color of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs changes from blue to green.

B-CDs exhibits excitation-independent behavior when excited light with a wavelength of 310–380 nm and also shows excitation-dependent behavior when wavelength changes between 380 nm and 480 nm (Fig. S2 a). And then C-CDs presents excitation-independent behavior, when the excitation wavelength is 310–390 nm and 400–480 nm (Fig. S2 b). Also, G-CDs shows excitation-dependent behavior as the increasing excitation wavelength from 350 to 480 nm (Fig. S2 c). It is said that uniform particle size distribution and/or the functional groups on the surface were contributed to the excitation-independent behavior. (Ke et al., 2016).

The QY of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs is calculated as 12.30 %, 16.17 % and 11.46 % respectively via Eq. (2). Moreover, as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S3, the time-resolved PL decay curve of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs are recorded and fitted. τ1 represents the short-lived component, which is attributed to the radiative recombination of intrinsic states. And τ2 is the long-lived component that corresponds to functional groups on the surface. The average lifetime of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs is 1.30, 1.81 and 2.61 ns respectively (Table S3). It is obvious that the average lifetime increases as the increase of HCL. In addition, the percentage of τ1 increases from 68.08 % to 97.10 % (Table S3). It indicates that the intrinsic states becomes more dominant in M-CDs with the increase of HCL.

Photostability of fluorescence materials is important for practical applications. Therefore, the photostability of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs did not change significantly after exposure to 365 nm UV light for 0, 60, and 90 min. It indicated that the prepared carbon dots had good photostability.

The morphology and structure of the prepared M-CDs were evaluated by transmission electron microscopy. As shown from the TEM images in Fig. 3, the particles of the three M-CDs are spherical and uniformly distributed in water with good dispersion. The average particle size of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs are 2.54 nm, 2.60 nm and 2.82 nm respectively, and the particle size distribution of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs are 1.6–3 nm, 2.0–3.5 nm and 1.6–4.4 nm respectively. It is notable that the emission wavelength of M-CDs redshifts (as discussed in 3.1) with the particle size increasing, which proves the prepared M-CDs in this study obeys the size-dependent effect. In addition, B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs possess lattice fringes spaced at 0.21 nm, corresponding to the [100] crystal plane of graphene (Zheng et al., 2021). Interestingly, G-CDs also exhibit another lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the [002] crystal plane of graphene (Zheng et al., 2021). It indicates that G-CDs has better crystallization than B-CDs and C-CDs.

XRD and Raman spectra are also used to investigate the crystallinity of the obtained B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4. In Fig. 4a, the XRD spectra of B-CDs and C-CDs have only one wide peak at 19.6°, which corresponds to the [100] plane of graphene. However, this peak of B-CDs and C-CDs is wider than that of pure graphene, which is due to the defects caused by functional groups in the carbon layers of M-CDs. Compared to B-CDs and C-CDs, the XRD spectrum of G-CDs exhibits three kinds of sharp peaks, of which the peak at 21.9° corresponds to the [100] plane of graphene, the peak at 26° is consistent with the [002] plane of graphene, and the peak at 32.6° are caused by the [001] plane of graphene (Zaib et al.). It demonstrates that G-CDs has better crystallization than B-CDs and C-CDs. This result is consistent with that of TEM image discussion in Fig. 3.

In Fig. 4b, three are two bands in the Raman spectra of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs. One is the D band near 1340 cm−1, which presents the disordered crystalline sp3 carbon structure (Atchudan et al., 2022). The other is the G band around 1630 cm−1 accounting for the graphitized sp2 carbon structure (Atchudan et al., 2020). With the increase of HCL load, the ratio of ID/IG of prepared M-CDs decreases from 1.30 (B-CDs) to 0.76 (C-CDs) and then to 0.52 (G-CDs), indicating that the degree of graphitization degree and conjugation degree of the M-CDs increases from B-CDs to C-CDs and then to G-CDs. It proves that the graphitization degree and conjugation degree of M-CDs can be enhanced by introducing HCL into the reaction system when lignin-based CDs are prepared by hydrothermal method.

The XPS of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs were studied for investigating their composition and valance state, as shown in Fig. 5. The full XPS spectra of B-CDs (Fig. 5a), C-CDs (Fig. 5b) and G-CDs (Fig. 5c) exhibit three peaks at 285 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV, which contribute to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. It reveals that all M-CDs contain the same elements (C, N, O), and indicates the successful incorporation of N atom into/ onto the carbon framework of M-CDs (Atchudan et al., 2019; Atchudan et al., 2018). Fig. 5d-l display the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of the M-CDs. The C 1s was divided into four binding energy peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, 287.3 eV and 288.8 eV for sp2 C (CC), sp3 C (C-O/C-N), CO and -COOH (O-CO), respectively (Zhao et al., 2021). The N 1s was also deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.8 eV and 402.2 eV, corresponding to amide N and graphitic N, respectively (Li et al., 2018b). And in the O 1s divided spectra, the peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.2 eV are fitted with CO and C-O, respectively (Zhao et al., 2021). According to the discussion above, all the M-CDs possess CC, C-O, C-N, CO, O-CO, amide N and graphitic N.

To reveal the relationship between surface state, QY and fluorescent emission shift of the M-CDs, the areas of the high resolution XPS peaks are measured and the results are shown in Table 1. For B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs, the ratio of N to C are 0.08, 0.12 and 0.03 respectively, and the QY are 12.30 %, 16.17 % and 11.46 %, respectively. It is indicated that for the prepared M-CDs, N content influences the QY of the M-CDs, and the higher N content, the higher QY. Furthermore, it is notable that graphitic N content of B-CDs, C-CDs and C-CDs are 0.09, 0.49 and to 0.66, respectively, suggesting that the more HCL load leads to a higher graphitization degree. This result is consistent to the discussion of XRD and Raman spectra in Fig. 4. Furthermore, It implies that the increase of sp2­conjugated domains size is related to the increase of the particle size and the redshifts in fluorescent emission. In addition, as shown in Table 1, the O/C ratios of B-CDs and G-CDs are 0.19, 0.20 and 0.22, respectively, indicating that with the increase of HCL load, the oxidation degree of M-CDs increases gradually. In brief, a conclusion can be drawn that the increase of N content in the structure of the M-CDs improves the its QY (Zheng et al., 2021). Besides, the red shift of the M-CDs is contributed to the increase of graphitization degree and the surface oxidation degree of the M-CDs. Those results are consistent with previous reports (Wang et al., 2020a). (Nguyen et al., 2020).

Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of the precursor of M-CDs (lignin), the prepared M-CDs and the hydrothermal reaction residues were also used to speculate functional groups and the structure of B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs, as shown in Fig. 6. All the M-CDs exhibit the peaks at 3211–3330 cm−1, 1488–1587 cm−1, 1309 cm−1, 1203 cm−1 and 1096–1147 cm−1, which is attribute to stretching vibration peaks of O-H/N-H (Wu et al., 2021), CC/CN (Guo et al., 2019), C-N (Wang et al., 2021), C-O (Liao et al., 2020) and C-O-C (Wang et al., 2021; Atchudan et al., 2016), respectively. However, an interesting phenomenon can be found by comparing the intensity of those peaks, that is, from B-CDs to C-CDs and then to G-CDs, the peak intensity of CC or CN increases sharply, indicating that the graphitization degree of the prepared M-CDs increases with the HCL load. Meanwhile, the intensity of C-N peak in the spectrum of C-CDs is sharper than that of B-CDs and G-CDs, indicating that the N content in C-CDs is higher than that of B-CDs or G-CDs. This result is consistent with the QY values of the three M-CDs in Table 1. In addition, it can also be obviously seen that the FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs has three different peaks at 2583–2814 cm−1 and 1616 cm−1, which are ascribed to the in-plane deformation vibrations of aromatic C-H group (Yang et al., 2020) and CO. It also means that compared with B-CDs and C-CDs, G-CDs has a higher aromatic structure and surface oxidation degree.

In conclusion, the results of XPS and FT-IR spectrum confirm each other, namely C-CDs possesses more N content than B-CDs and G-CDs, and G-CDs and has the most aromatic structure and surface oxidation degree.

As shown in Fig. 1, Fig. S1 and Table S1, the fluorescent colors of the M-CDs change from blue, cyan and light green to green as the amount of HCL increases. It is obviously that the immediate cause for this phenomenon is the changes of the amount of HCL. However, the root cause is the different depolymerization degrees of the lignin. Based on the results above and the report (Long et al., 2014) about the hydrothermal depolymerization of lignin, a probable formation mechanism of the M-CDs was found in this study.

Although there were three different M-CDs derived from lignin, the process of the formation could actually be divided into four parts: structural collapse, bond breaking and transfer, repolymerization and carbonization. Firstly, as the temperature rised to 180 °C and remained stable, the structure of lignin starts to collapse due to the breaking of lignin’s hydrogen bonds. Secondly, under the action of thermal effect, easily broken bonds in lignin begin to break, like the β-O-4 bond. Then the lignin is cleaved to produce some fragments such as volatile aromatic products and oligomers. In this period, the exist of HCL can make the β-O-4 bond break more easily so that more aromatic products and oligomers can be produced. Therefore, more and more aromatic products and oligomers will involved in the next period of the reaction, which causes the difference in M-CDs. Thirdly, some of the unsaturated aromatic oligomers are then repolymerized with the production of solids, which can not be depolymerized and become the residues. As shown in Fig. 6b, the spectra of the lignin and the residues were almost the same, which confirmed that those residues still retained the characteristic structure of lignin. Then the depolymerization and repolymerization take place simultaneously at the forth period. At the same time, the MPDA take part in the reaction to provide the nitrogen groups. As the reaction time increases, the carbon core is gradually formed after polymerization and carbonization. Finally, the M-CDs are successfully synthesized. In conclusion, HCL can make the lignin easy to depolymerize to produce more lignin aromatic fragments and it means that there are enough aromatic molecules to be carbonized for the formation of M-CDs with larger particle size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FA by colorimetric method
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7a demonstrates the process of formaldehyde detection with B-CDs probe. As shown in Fig. 7b, B-CDs solution becomes increasingly turbid under sunlight with the FA concentration increasing from 0 to 1.0 mg/L. When the FA concentration is between 0 and 0.2 mg/L, B-CDs solution keeps clear. But when the FA concentration is between 0.4 and 1 mg/L, B-CDs solution becomes turbid gradually which is due to the increase of Schiff base formed by the reaction between CDs and FA with the increase of FA concentration. Without UV irradiation, the above experimental phenomenon can be obviously observed with naked eyes under sunlight, which simplifies the FA detection procedure and makes the detection faster and easier. Especially, when the concentration of FA is less than 0.4 mg/L, the turbidity does not occur in the mixed solution of B-CDs with FA (B-CDs-FA solution), indicating good potential of B-CDs in colorimetric probe for FA detection. According to China's drinking water quality standard, the concentration limit of FA in drinking water is 0.9 mg/L. Therefore, this study proposes a very simple method to detect FA in drinking water by adding drinking water samples into the prepared B-CDs solution (Fig. 7a). If the solution becomes turbid, it means that the FA content in drinking water exceeds China's drinking water quality standard, which is harmful to human health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FA by fluorometric method
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8a comparatively shows the photos of B-CDs-FA mixed solution under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp after adding FA with the concentration of 1 mg/L. The fluorescent intensity of B-CDs decreases significantly after adding with 1 mg/L FA, indicating that FA can cause the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs. To more accurately reflect the response of B-CDs to FA, Fig. 8b lists the emission spectra of B-CDs-FA with different concentration of FA at 380 nm UV excitation. The center of fluorescence emission spectrum of all B-CDs-FA solutions is located at about 493 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs-FA gradually decreases with the increase of FA concentration, which is verified with the result in Fig. 8a. Therefore, it can be concluded that B-CDs prepared in this study has good sensitivity to FA with concentrations of 0 mg/L-1 mg/L. Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 8c, the relationship between F/F0 and concentration displays a good linear with concentration of FA range from 0 mg/L to 1 mg/L. The linear fitting equation was calculated as y = 163.76x + 0.9638 (R2 = 0.9834). The LOD of the B-CDs toward FA was estimated to be 7.40 μg/L according to the Eq. (2), demonstrating the great potential of B-CDs as a fluorescent probe for FA detection.

Compared with the FA probes reported previously, the performance of B-CDs in this study is relatively good, such as a low detection limit and a good linear range. However, the biggest advantage of the probe in this study is that it can resolve the FA concentration in sunlight, without the use of UV excitation. The B-CDs probe is simple to use and has a wide range of raw materials.

To further reveal the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs by FA, the Uv-vis absorption spectra of B-CDs and B-CDs-FA solution were comparatively studied in Fig. 8d. The spectrum of B-CDs-FA is basically consistent with that of B-CDs, with the same absorption peaks at 230 nm and 290 nm, which are attribute to π-π * transitions of CC bond, and n-π * transitions of CO bond, respectively. This indicates that the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs by adding formaldehyde is not caused by changing the fluorescence groups in B-CDs. Then, why does adding a certain concentration of formaldehyde to B-CDs lead to its fluorescence quenching? Let's look at Fig. 7 again. As formaldehyde concentration increases from 0 to 1 mg/L, the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs can be divided into two sections. In the first section, when FA concentration is increased from 0 mg/L to 0.2 mg/L, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs-FA decreased slowly, and the formation of Schiff base is not observed, as shown in Fig. 8b. In the second section, when the formaldehyde concentration exceeds 0.4 mg/L, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs decreases sharply with the increase of FA concentration added to B-CDs, and obviously Schiff base precipitation can be observed. The formation of Schiff base influences the electronic states and nonradiative transitions, which makes the intramolecular charge transfer weaker and leads to the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs. This mechanism is similar to that mentioned in Liu’s and Li’s work. (Liu et al., 2019), (Li et al., 2018a).

Fig. 9a shows the changes of B-CDs solution and fluorescence under 380 nm UV excitation after K+, Zn2+, Na+, Ca2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ were added into B-CDs solution. As shown in Fig. 9a, after the above ions were added, there was no obvious change in the surface of B-CDs solution, nor turbidity of the solution. Moreover, when excited by 380 nm UV light, B-CDs solution with different ions mentioned above did not have fluorescence quench, compared with formaldehyde which had obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of B-CDs. This experimental phenomenon shows that B-CDs prepared in this study has good selectivity to formaldehyde and can effectively detect formaldehyde in water.

In addition, to eliminate the interference of pH environment in water, the stability of B-CDs under different pH conditions were studied, as shown in Fig. 9c. When pH is 3–9, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs hardly changed. Considering that the actual drinking water is almost neutral, we believe that the B-CDs formaldehyde sensor can effectively resist the interference of pH environment in water.

A series of lignin derived multicolor carbon dots from blue to green were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. Multi fluorescent emission of M-CDs was realized by adjusting depolymerization degree of lignin with different amount of HCL in hydrothermal process. With the increase of HCL content, lignin depolymerized with HCL to form more aromatic molecules, resulting in the formation of more sp2 structure in the carbon core. The increase in average particle size, graphitization degree and surface oxidation degree of M-CDs led to the redshift of the fluorescent emission wavelength of M-CDs, and the quantum yield (QY) of M-CDs was improved by the increase of N content in M-CDs. Among the series of multicolor carbon dots from blue to cyan and to green emission, the probe based on B-CDs exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity towards FA. It can detect FA by observing with naked eyes in sunlight. When the concentration of FA was more than 0.4 mg/L, the B-CDs solution became turbid, which meant the water sample contained excessive levels of formaldehyde. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity of B-CDs can be quenched by FA, and it correlated linearly with FA concentration from 0.02 mg/L to 1 mg/L with a LOD of 7.40 μg/L. Thus, this study provides a simple method to prepare the multi-emission lignin derived CDs with high sensitivity for FA detection through a dual-mode method.

Ying Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Kai Liu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Miaoyan Hu: Visualization, Investigation. Hailan Lian: Supervision. Shiyu Gao: Software, Validation. Changyan Xu: Writing – review & editing.
